items.title: Natural Selection Requires Variation, Inheritance, and Differential Survival
items.body: Darwin's Origin, 1859 first edition, supports the core Notebook model of natural selection: more organisms are born than can survive, heritable variation exists, and variants profitable under local conditions tend to persist and propagate.

review.notes: Foundational source for the selection-foundations slot; pair with Mayr/Pianka for modern population-genetic framing.

items.title: Natural Selection Is Differential Reproduction, Not A Purposeful Selector
items.body: Dobzhansky, Ayala, Stebbins, and Valentine support a guardrail that natural selection is an outcome of organism-environment interactions and differential reproduction, not a purposeful chooser and not necessarily literal combat among organisms.

review.notes: Promote as a misconception guard for natural selection and adaptation pages.

items.title: The Synthetic Theory Connects Selection With Multiple Biological Fields
items.body: Dobzhansky, Ayala, Stebbins, and Valentine support presenting modern evolutionary theory as a synthesis that joins Darwinian selection with genetics and is continually expanded by ecology, systematics, molecular biology, and paleobiology.

review.notes: Useful as an overview bridge from the first-ring concepts into later molecular, ecological, and fossil-evidence paths.

items.title: Hard Selection Can Resemble Artificial Selection
items.body: Futuyma's response-to-selection chapter supports a bridge between artificial selection and some natural-selection cases: when survival depends on possessing a specific character state, hard selection can resemble an experimenter selecting a trait threshold.

review.notes: Use as a higher-level natural-selection record after learners have basic differential reproduction. It should not imply that all natural selection is hard selection or experimentally threshold-like.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: Hard selection can be modeled as differential survival or reproduction against an absolute environmental threshold, while artificial selection imposes an externally chosen threshold or ranking on a measured trait.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: conceptualized; no equation preserved in this sidecar
assumptions: the selected character state can be identified
fitness differences are tied to survival or reproduction
the comparison is about model structure, not identical causal origin
soft-selection cases remain a separate context-dependent regime
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: The same trait-response logic can illuminate artificial selection and some natural-selection cases, but only when the selection regime is made explicit.

items.title: Soft Selection Is Context-Relative Replacement
items.body: Futuyma's treatment of genetic load supports a distinction between hard and soft selection: in hard selection, unconditionally inferior genotypes can lower population density, while in soft selection, relatively inferior genotypes are replaced under density regulation, so selection need not appear as an ecological increase in population size or mean density.

review.notes: Use as the missing explanatory bridge for the existing soft/hard selection coverage probe. Keep beginner-facing copy focused on the contrast between absolute genotype disadvantage and context-relative replacement.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: Hard selection ties genotype-dependent survival or reproduction to absolute population loss; soft selection changes genotype frequencies through relative success under density regulation or other ecological constraints.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: conceptualized from prose and genetic-load argument; no equation preserved in this sidecar
assumptions: genotypes can differ in survival or fecundity
population density may be limited by ecological factors independent of the selected locus
relative genotype replacement can change allele frequencies without increasing total density
genetic load and substitutional load arguments depend on whether selective deaths are independent and absolute
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: The same allele-frequency change can have different demographic meaning depending on whether selection is absolute or context-relative.

items.title: Selection Claims Need Trait and Fitness Evidence
items.body: A frequency increase alone does not establish that an allele is adaptive; the explanation also needs evidence connecting the variant to heritable trait differences, fitness differences, and relevant environmental conditions.

review.notes: Pianka OCR import anchors the distinction between selection, evolution, alternative mechanisms, and caution against circular adaptive explanations. Keep citation-specific page text out of public pages until final bibliography review.

items.title: Selection Requires Heritable Phenotypic Differences
items.body: Pianka's genetics chapter supports the Notebook guardrail that selection acts on phenotypes, but population change depends on whether phenotypic differences are heritable.

review.notes: Useful for distinguishing immediate fitness effects from evolutionary response. OCR section heading is noisy, but the page anchor is usable.

items.title: Wallace Frames Selection Through Environmental Constraints
items.body: Wallace's 1858 paper gives the Notebook a historical bridge for natural selection: wild populations are constrained by food, enemies, and changing conditions, so variants that improve persistence can increase and sometimes diverge from parent forms.

review.notes: Use as a historical source sidecar for natural selection and adaptation; the wording avoids claiming Wallace used later population-genetic terminology.

items.title: Industrial Melanism Shows Directional Selection On Allele Frequencies
items.body: Futuyma uses industrial melanism in peppered moths as a directional-selection example: a melanic form increased from very low frequency to dominance in some populations when predation favored darker moths in polluted habitats.

review.notes: Good example for linking natural selection to allele-frequency change; use modern caveats if expanding into a full page treatment.

items.title: Resource Allocation As An Optimization Model
items.body: Wilson's resource-allocation chapter can support a web-facing example where alternative allocation schedules vary, reproduce according to performance, and converge toward higher-fitness strategies under specified environmental assumptions.

review.notes: Use as a natural-selection/modeling bridge. The genetic algorithm is an optimization device for exploring strategy space, not evidence that real populations literally run that algorithm.

algorithm_translation.translation_policy: clean-room genetic-algorithm-style demonstration of selection among allocation schedules
algorithm_translation.model_focus: fitness-mediated search over resource allocation schedules

copyright_review.risk_reducer: independent JavaScript model with explicit assumptions and tests

expected_output_checks: Changing the reproduction threshold should change convergence speed or stability.
Proportional reproduction should be compared with threshold reproduction as a model assumption.
The UI should expose the fitness function separately from the search procedure.

items.title: Why Does Darwin Edition Provenance Matter?
items.body: A learner cites Darwin's Origin for natural selection and quotes wording from the sixth edition, including the phrase 'survival of the fittest.' Why should the Notebook record the exact edition and local file instead of treating every Origin citation as the 1859 first edition?

review.notes: This item is a provenance guardrail, not a new definition of natural selection. It exists to prevent edition drift in Darwin-sourced Notebook claims.

expected_answer_path: state that Darwin's Origin exists in multiple editions with additions and corrections
recognize that later wording can differ from the 1859 first edition
use the sixth-edition source trail when citing sixth-edition language such as 'survival of the fittest'
avoid attributing edition-specific wording to the first-edition source trail
record exact local file, edition, volume year, and anchor before promoting a claim
coverage_probe.coverage_note: This strengthens source-trail operations for Darwin source-family ingestion.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: A Notebook source claim must bind concept, wording, local file, edition, year, and locator; if edition differs, the source_id differs.
assumptions: multiple local Darwin Origin texts may be available
wording and framing can vary across editions
teaching claims should be traceable to the exact text used
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: Accurate source provenance keeps historical wording from being mistaken for a timeless or edition-neutral formulation of evolutionary theory.

source_context.source_section: Project Gutenberg header; sixth-edition title note; Chapter III, Struggle for Existence
source_context.edition_or_volume_year: 1872 sixth edition; Project Gutenberg otoos610.txt local etext

items.title: How Is Sexual Selection Different From Survival Selection?
items.body: A male trait increases mating success because it helps its bearer defeat rivals or attract mates, but the same trait is costly for ordinary survival. How should this be classified, and why is it not enough to ask only whether the trait helps the individual stay alive?

review.notes: Paraphrased from Darwin's Descent treatment of sexual selection. Use as a mechanism-distinction question rather than as an endorsement of every historical example or social claim in the source.

expected_answer_path: identify the trait as a sexual-selection case when its advantage is differential mating or fertilization success
distinguish same-sex competition from mate choice as two routes by which sexual selection can act
contrast reproductive success with ordinary survival under the general conditions of life
explain that a trait can persist when mating advantages offset survival costs
recognize that natural selection can still constrain sexually selected traits if their survival costs become too high
coverage_probe.coverage_note: This adds a Darwin-source coverage probe for reproductive-success mechanisms within the natural-selection concept page.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: Total evolutionary success can depend on survival and reproduction; sexual selection isolates variation in reproductive success among same-sex competitors or mate-attraction alternatives.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: qualitative primary-source passage normalized as a fitness-component distinction
assumptions: the trait varies among individuals
trait differences affect mating or fertilization success
the reproductive advantage is heritable enough to change descendant frequencies
ordinary survival costs and reproductive benefits are evaluated together
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: Selection does not act only through staying alive; differences in access to mates can also change which heritable variants are represented in later generations.

source_context.source_section: Part II, Principles of Sexual Selection; General Summary and Conclusion
source_context.textbook_prompt_role: primary-source concept passage converted into a modern coverage question
source_context.edition_or_volume_year: 1871 work; Project Gutenberg dscmn10.txt local etext

items.title: What Does Gene-Centered Selection Explain?
items.body: A trait affects organism survival and reproduction, but Dawkins frames selection in terms of heritable variants that become more common through differential reproduction. How can this gene-centered view help explain evolutionary change, and what should it not make you forget about organisms, populations, development, and ecological context?

review.notes: Paraphrased from local OCR of the 1976 split-PDF scan. Use as a guardrail question: gene-centered selection is a useful level-of-selection model, not a claim that genes have conscious motives or that organismal phenotypes, populations, development, and ecology can be ignored.

expected_answer_path: define natural selection as non-random differential reproduction of heritable variants
explain that gene-centered framing tracks what persists through inheritance across generations
distinguish gene-centered selection from unsupported group-benefit explanations
explain that organisms remain the interacting phenotypes through which gene effects are expressed
state that population structure, development, ecology, and environment still shape selection outcomes
avoid treating selfish as a conscious motive, moral claim, or complete explanation by itself
coverage_probe.coverage_note: This fills a natural-selection guardrail around levels of selection while preventing a common overextension of gene-centered language.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: If a heritable variant g affects its expected representation in future generations, selection can be described by changes in the frequency of g, while organismal phenotypes and ecological interactions mediate those effects.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: qualitative source normalized as an inheritance-frequency model
assumptions: heritable variation exists
variants differ in expected representation across generations
phenotypes mediate many gene effects
population and ecological context affect fitness outcomes
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: Gene-centered selection can clarify what is inherited and amplified, but the causal path usually runs through organisms in populations and environments.

source_context.source_section: The Selfish Gene, chapter 1 and transition to chapter 2
source_context.textbook_prompt_role: canonical source framing converted into a coverage question

items.title: Does Heterozygote Success Prove Overdominance?
items.body: A study reports that heterozygotes at an enzyme marker have higher fecundity than homozygotes, and the heterozygote frequency rises over several generations. What evidence would be needed before concluding that selection is maintaining a single-locus overdominant polymorphism rather than a linked or multi-locus effect?

review.notes: Paraphrased from Futuyma's treatment of heterozygous advantage and pseudo-overdominance. Keep as an evidence-quality question: observed heterozygote performance can motivate a selection hypothesis, but it does not by itself isolate the causal locus.

expected_answer_path: state that higher heterozygote fecundity or increasing heterozygosity is consistent with heterozygote advantage
explain why the marker might be linked to causal loci rather than itself overdominant
identify pseudo-overdominance as a pattern caused by linkage disequilibrium or linked deleterious recessives being masked in hybrids
ask for recombination, breeding, mapping, or genotype-fitness evidence that separates the marker from neighboring loci
distinguish single-locus overdominance from multi-locus heterosis, chromosomal inversions, and environment-averaged marginal overdominance
coverage_probe.coverage_note: This strengthens balancing-selection coverage by adding a causal-inference guardrail around overdominance claims.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: Single-locus overdominance requires w_AA' > w_AA and w_AA' > w_A'A' for the causal locus itself; marker heterozygote superiority is insufficient if marker genotype is correlated with linked causal variants.
assumptions: fitness differences are estimated for genotypes with adequate replication
marker genotypes can be separated from linked background effects
linkage disequilibrium and recombination are considered explicitly
environmental heterogeneity and multi-locus heterosis are competing explanations
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: Selection can maintain polymorphism by heterozygote advantage, but proving the mechanism requires evidence that the heterozygote advantage belongs to the focal locus rather than to the chromosomal or genetic background it tags.

notation_mapping.meaning: three genotypes at a focal locus; OCR and source typography vary
apparent heterozygote superiority caused by linkage disequilibrium or linked multi-locus effects rather than intrinsic superiority at one locus

source_context.source_section: The Effects of Selection on Gene Frequencies; Heterozygous Advantage; For Discussion and Thought
source_context.textbook_prompt_role: end-of-chapter interpretation question expanded with nearby explanatory passage

items.title: Why Does Realized Heritability Change During Selection?
items.body: If realized heritability is estimated from the response to selection divided by the selection differential, why might it decline or change as artificial selection continues across generations?

review.notes: Mined from an end-of-section textbook question. Keep as an advanced quantitative-genetics coverage probe rather than a beginner-route item.

expected_answer_path: define response to selection R and selection differential S
explain realized heritability as R divided by S in the exercise framing
connect declining response to depletion or rearrangement of selectable genetic variation
distinguish genetic variation from immediately selectable genetic variation
note that changing environments, linkage, dominance, and correlated fitness costs can alter response
coverage_probe.coverage_note: This adds a quantitative-genetics bridge between heritability, artificial selection, and evolutionary response.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: Realized heritability can be estimated as h_realized^2 = R / S, where R is the response to selection and S is the selection differential.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: normalized from OCR; source symbol for heritability is noisy
assumptions: trait values are measured consistently across generations
selection differential and response are defined on comparable scales
response reflects heritable variation as well as other genetic and environmental constraints
the relation is an empirical estimate, not a guarantee of constant future response
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: The equation turns a breeding or selection experiment into an estimate of how much selectable inheritance remains visible in the population.

notation_mapping.meaning: estimated heritability from response divided by selection differential

items.title: Soft Selection And Hard Selection
items.body: How would soft selection and hard selection differ in their effects on fertility, viability, and the rate of evolutionary response?

review.notes: Reviewed against local OCR from the scanned Futuyma volume. Keep as an advanced coverage probe: it asks about selection regime, genetic homeostasis, fertility/viability decline, and evolutionary rate.

expected_answer_path: definition of soft selection
definition of hard selection
comparison of density- or context-dependent fitness effects
explain why fertility or viability decline may mark genetic homeostasis under some regimes
compare expected evolutionary rates under context-dependent versus absolute-threshold selection
coverage_probe.coverage_note: This question can now be paired with the hard-selection/artificial-selection bridge record and the soft-selection density-regulated replacement claim.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: Compare evolutionary response under context-relative soft selection with response under absolute-threshold hard selection, especially where fertility, viability, and selectable genetic variance change across generations.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: conceptualized; no equation preserved in this sidecar
assumptions: fitness effects can be context-dependent or threshold-like
fertility and viability can change as correlated responses
evolutionary rate depends on selectable variation and selection regime
genetic homeostasis is treated as a hypothesis to explain limited or plateaued response
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: The exercise makes selection regime part of the explanation for why similar trait differences may yield different evolutionary rates.

items.title: Did Longer Survival Mean Higher Fitness?
items.body: An organism with one trait survives longer, while another leaves more successful offspring. Which one has higher evolutionary fitness in this setting, and what measurements would you need to justify the answer?

review.notes: Misconception check for equating fitness with longevity, strength, or survival alone.

items.title: Why Is One Species More Abundant?
items.body: Wallace asks learners to explain why closely related species can differ greatly in abundance even when both reproduce rapidly. How should the explanation connect population checks, food supply, enemies, and useful variation rather than fecundity alone?

review.notes: Paraphrased from Wallace's 1858 argument. Keep historical phrasing separate from later population-genetic terms; use this as a foundation-ring assessment prompt.

expected_answer_path: explain why fecundity alone does not determine abundance
identify environmental checks such as food supply, enemies, and unfavorable seasons
connect population limitation to differential persistence among individuals or allied species
explain why useful variations should tend to increase while harmful ones diminish
avoid projecting later genetic terminology onto Wallace's historical argument
coverage_probe.coverage_note: This adds a Wallace-derived historical assessment path for natural selection and adaptation.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: When reproduction can exceed replacement but population size remains bounded, differential persistence under environmental checks can change the representation of variants or allied forms.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: conceptualized; no modern allele-frequency notation is imposed on the historical source
assumptions: organisms reproduce in excess of long-term replacement
food supply, enemies, and seasonal conditions limit survival
variants can differ in traits affecting persistence
historical natural-selection arguments can be used without translating them into modern genetics
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: Wallace's argument links abundance, scarcity, and divergence to fit with conditions of existence rather than to reproductive output alone.

source_context.source_section: The Struggle for Existence; The Law of Population of Species; Useful Variations

items.title: When Does The Lottery Analogy For Sex Work?
items.body: Williams compares sexually produced propagules to holding several different lottery tickets rather than many copies of one ticket. In the aphid-rotifer model, what ecological and life-history conditions make that analogy a plausible selective advantage for sex?

review.notes: Paraphrased from Williams's aphid-rotifer model. Use as an assumption-check question: the model should not be generalized to all sex without the ecological and life-history structure that makes the lottery analogy meaningful.

expected_answer_path: identify heterogonic life cycles with repeated asexual generations and occasional sexual production of widely dispersed propagules
specify small or discontinuous habitats that can be saturated by a clone
explain why many copies of one asexual genotype may be redundant if one clone can dominate the local habitat
explain why sexually produced propagules can spread a parent's genes across multiple genotypes, increasing the chance that one clone is locally best
include strong within-habitat competition and enough clonal generations for small fitness differences to change clone abundance
state caveats: high between-habitat heritability of fitness or weak local competition would reduce the advantage
avoid treating the lottery analogy as a general proof that sex is always favored
coverage_probe.coverage_note: This deepens the Williams 1975 sex-and-recombination coverage by requiring students to connect the benefit of variation to explicit ecological assumptions.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: Sex can be favored in the aphid-rotifer model when diverse sexual propagules increase the probability that at least one descendant genotype wins local clonal competition enough to offset the cost of meiosis.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: source symbols normalized qualitatively; Williams uses m and n for established asexual and sexual propagules, t for clonal generations, and lambda for mean propagule number in simulations
assumptions: colonized habitats receive a limited, nonrandom sample of propagules
one successful clone can saturate or dominate the habitat
sexual propagules differ genetically while asexual propagules copy the parental genotype
within-habitat fitness differences have higher predictive value than between-habitat fitness heritability
clonal competition lasts long enough for fitness differences to affect relative abundance
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: The benefit of sex comes from distributing genetic representation across alternative local competitors when local success is winner-weighted, not from variation as an abstract good.

notation_mapping.meaning: number or average number of established asexual propagules in a colonized habitat
number or average number of established sexual propagules in a colonized habitat
number of asexual generations of competition within the colonized habitat
mean propagule number in the Williams and Mitton simulation

source_context.textbook_prompt_role: canonical source model converted into a coverage question
source_context.edition_or_volume_year: 1975 monograph; local scan of third printing with corrections, 1977

items.title: What Must A Benefit Of Sex Overcome?
items.body: Williams frames sexual reproduction as a paradox because outcrossed sex can halve a parent's direct genetic representation compared with an asexual alternative. If a student says sex is favored simply because recombination creates variation, what additional evidence or model conditions are needed before that explanation is adequate?

review.notes: Paraphrased from Williams's opening paradox and later recombination discussion. Use as a guardrail against hand-waving from 'variation is good' to a sufficient adaptive explanation for sex.

expected_answer_path: state the cost being explained: compared with an asexual alternative, outcrossed sex can transmit only half of a parent's genes through each sexual offspring
separate the existence of genetic variation from an explanation of why sex is favored despite the cost
ask whether the proposed benefit is large enough to offset the cost of meiosis and any recombinational load
look for life-history or ecological conditions such as uncertain environments, intense juvenile selection, colonization, or progeny fitness variance that can make diverse offspring immediately useful
avoid relying only on long-term species benefits or vague group-selection claims unless the model explains how selection maintains sex in reproducing individuals or stable mixed populations
recognize that recombination can matter under some population-genetic conditions, but those conditions must be specified rather than assumed
coverage_probe.coverage_note: This adds an advanced natural-selection probe for the cost of sex and for evaluating whether a proposed adaptive explanation has the right scale and mechanism.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: A sexual-reproduction advantage A is sufficient only if A offsets the direct genetic-transmission cost C and any recombinational load L; qualitatively, selection can favor sex when A > C + L under the relevant life-history and population-genetic assumptions.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: source argument normalized as a threshold model
assumptions: an asexual alternative is physiologically available or is the relevant comparison
outcrossed sexual reproduction transmits one of two maternal homologs through a given offspring
benefits of variation, recombination, or dispersal are evaluated as effects on expected descendant production
population size, mutation supply, selection intensity, and ecological uncertainty can change whether recombination or diverse progeny is advantageous
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: Sex is not explained by saying that variation exists; the explanation must show why a lineage gains enough immediate or modeled expected success to compensate for the genetic-transmission cost.

source_context.source_section: Chapter 1, An Important Question, Its Easy Answer, and the Consequent Paradox; Chapter 12, Sex as a Factor in Organic Evolution
source_context.textbook_prompt_role: canonical source argument converted into a coverage question
source_context.edition_or_volume_year: 1975 monograph; local scan of third printing with corrections, 1977

items.title: When Are Identical Propagules Redundant?
items.body: Williams's elm-oyster model treats enormous numbers of seedlings or larvae competing for adult space as a setting where many copies of one genotype may be wasteful. What conditions would make genetically diverse sexual offspring a better investment than asexual copies in this model?

review.notes: Paraphrased from Williams's elm-oyster model. Use as a threshold/model question: students should connect the advantage of sex to high fecundity, strong juvenile selection, and capped adult-space winnings rather than to variation as a generic benefit.

expected_answer_path: identify high fecundity: many seeds, larvae, seedlings, or spat enter a space that can support far fewer adults
state that juvenile competition is genetically selective rather than wholly random
explain that adult habitat has a ceiling on success, so many copies of the same winning genotype do not multiply the adult-space prize
connect sexual reproduction to higher variance among offspring genotypes, increasing the chance that at least one offspring is among the fittest few
recognize that the benefit must exceed the cost of meiosis before sex is favored
include spatial clustering or limited suitable settlement surfaces as conditions that make redundant stocking plausible
avoid applying the model where offspring are not crowded, competition is mostly nonselective, or extra copies of the same genotype produce proportional returns
coverage_probe.coverage_note: This adds a Williams 1975 model-threshold probe for high-fecundity selection and redundant stocking of capped adult habitat.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: When N juvenile propagules compete for K adult spaces with K much smaller than N, extra copies of one genotype have diminishing returns; sexual reproduction is favored if increased genotype variance raises expected capped success by more than the meiotic cost.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: source model normalized; Williams uses propagule number, local/general fitness dosage, and habitat occupancy in simulations
assumptions: juvenile propagule density is high relative to adult habitat capacity
adult space or resource capture imposes a ceiling on success for any one genotype
competition among juveniles has a genetic component
sexual offspring have greater variance in total fitness dosage than identical asexual offspring
offspring from a parent can be sufficiently clustered or numerous that duplicate genotypes compete for the same capped prize
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: Sex can be favored when the limiting outcome is not how many copies are present, but whether one descendant genotype wins a crowded local contest for adult space.

source_context.textbook_prompt_role: canonical source model converted into a coverage question
source_context.edition_or_volume_year: 1975 monograph; local scan of third printing with corrections, 1977

items.title: Fitness Is Not One Lucky Outcome
items.body: An individual with an obvious survival impairment leaves more descendants than healthier members of its group because a chance disaster kills most of the others. Why should that lucky outcome not, by itself, make the impaired individual the fittest member of the population?

review.notes: Paraphrased from Williams's horse-with-a-broken-leg example. Use as a misconception guard separating expected adaptive design from single-case realized reproductive success.

expected_answer_path: distinguish expected fitness from realized reproductive outcome in one case
state that chance can affect which individuals leave descendants
connect fitness judgments to survival and reproductive design relative to the population and environment
avoid defining fitness solely by one observed success after an accident
explain that repeated patterns across many cases are needed to evaluate whether the fitness judgment is wrong
coverage_probe.coverage_note: This adds a Williams-backed natural-selection probe for stochastic outcomes and expected versus realized fitness.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: Fitness should be treated as a probabilistic expectation over comparable cases, while realized descendant counts can be altered by chance events in individual cases.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: conceptualized; source argument is verbal rather than equation-based
assumptions: individuals differ in traits affecting expected survival or reproduction
environmental accidents can change realized survival independently of those traits
fitness claims are evaluated relative to a population and ecological context
single observations are insufficient to estimate expectation reliably
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: Natural selection depends on biased differences in expected survival and reproduction, not on declaring every lucky survivor adaptively superior.

items.title: What Would Make Extinction Evidence Count As Group Selection?
items.body: A textbook says a trait persisted because groups or taxa with that trait were less likely to go extinct. Using Williams's criteria, what evidence would be needed before treating that as a serious group-selection explanation rather than an after-the-fact extinction story?

review.notes: Paraphrased from Williams's chapter on sex as a factor in biotic evolution. Use as a guardrail against casual group-selection and extinction-adaptation explanations.

expected_answer_path: distinguish organic evolution within populations from biotic evolution in the composition of a biota
state the proposed group-selection claim in terms of differential persistence or extinction among groups or taxa
require evidence that extinction risk is consistently biased by the trait or life-cycle feature across comparable cases
separate a plausible story for one extinction from a predictive basis for many extinctions
recognize that local adaptation can increase fit to a niche that later disappears
avoid treating extinction frequency, extinction causes, or long-term benefit language as sufficient evidence for group selection
coverage_probe.coverage_note: This adds a Williams 1975 evidence-standard prompt for selection-level claims and adaptation/extinction guardrails.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: A group-selection explanation requires a repeatable association between group-level trait state and differential group persistence, not merely individual-level adaptation plus observed extinction.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: conceptual criterion normalized from source prose
assumptions: groups or taxa are comparable units for the proposed claim
trait states can be measured independently of the extinction outcome
extinction or persistence is assessed across multiple cases or with a predictive contrast
within-population selection and among-group persistence are not conflated
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: Selection-level explanations need evidence at the level where the claimed sorting occurs; after-the-fact extinction narratives do not by themselves establish group selection.

source_context.source_section: Chapter 13, Sex as a Factor in Biotic Evolution
source_context.textbook_prompt_role: canonical source argument converted into an evidence-standard question
source_context.edition_or_volume_year: 1975 monograph; local scan of third printing with corrections, 1977

items.title: How Could Heavy Juvenile Mortality Be Selective?
items.body: In a high-fecundity population, most seedlings, larvae, or juveniles die before adulthood. What evidence would let you argue that this mortality is genetically selective rather than mostly random loss, and why does that matter for natural selection?

review.notes: Paraphrased from Williams's high-fecundity evidence chapters. Use as an evidence-design question: the point is not that all juvenile mortality is selective, but that selection cannot be dismissed without checking viability, fertility, genetic load, and gene-frequency evidence.

expected_answer_path: measure whether juvenile survival differs consistently with size, vigor, stress resistance, infection resistance, or other adaptive performance traits
ask whether the measured performance differences have a genetic component
look for genetic load or inbreeding depression as evidence that low-viability genotypes are present
compare gene frequencies across nearby localities or age cohorts where dispersal should otherwise homogenize frequencies
include fertility variation as a fitness component, not only viability
avoid assuming high mortality is nonselective merely because it is numerically large or occurs early
coverage_probe.coverage_note: This adds a Williams 1975 evidence-design probe for high-fecundity selection, linking mortality, genetic variation, and fertility to natural-selection inference.

mathematical_structure.kind: selection inference from mortality and allele-frequency evidence
mathematical_structure.formal_statement: Heavy juvenile mortality supports a natural-selection inference only when survival or fertility differences are associated with heritable variation, or when allele-frequency differences exceed what dispersal and drift plausibly explain.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: conceptual criterion normalized from source prose
assumptions: juvenile mortality is measured across comparable cohorts or environments
performance or fertility differences can be linked to heritable variation
dispersal and isolation are considered before inferring selection from spatial gene-frequency gradients
fitness includes both viability and fertility components
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: In high-fecundity organisms, enormous mortality can produce strong selection if deaths are biased among genotypes; the empirical task is to show the bias rather than assume it.

source_context.source_section: Chapters 7-8, Natural Selection in High-fecundity Populations: Evidence on Viability and Fertility
source_context.textbook_prompt_role: canonical source evidence converted into a coverage question
source_context.edition_or_volume_year: 1975 monograph; local scan of third printing with corrections, 1977
